title: So what is the significance of kid Shinji holding a core
display_title: So what is the significance of kid Shinji holding a core
wikitext:
[]: So what is the significance of kid-Shinji holding a core?             Quote Post by FallenTabris » Wed Mar 11, 2009 4:02 am I'm speaking of ep. 16, right before 01 breaks free. Shinji's shown holding a miniature core. Yui says something to the effect of "Good for you", and Rei mimes it when he wakes up. I never understood the significance of this statement, or why Shinji's holding a mini-core.

[]:             Quote Post by Oedipusfoot » Wed Mar 11, 2009 5:26 am In the cel drafts it's labeled as a marble. I've always wondered this too. Maybe it's without meaning but it just serves as an image that would remind us of 'a core.' Though when I saw that I would always say "Lilith's egg!" but who knows.

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Wed Mar 11, 2009 6:05 am Maybe not a core, but the little red dot that represents a soul (as seen in EoE)? Then it would be, "Look Mom! I found your soul", meaning at this point, Shinji knows who's inside Eva-01. Note the similarity of this scene to the one in EoE before Asuka realizes who was in Eva-02.

[]:             Quote Post by FallenTabris » Wed Mar 11, 2009 6:05 am maybe the implication is he found it in the sandbox you see him playing with the other children in in EoE (I think it's safe to assume that sequence was an alteration of an actual memory, much like the Asuka strangling scene), but again, not sure of the significance.

[]:             Quote Post by ZapX » Wed Mar 11, 2009 6:06 am I remember the "Look, mom! Found your soul!" thread. Whether it was on here or ANF escapes me.

[]:             Quote Post by FallenTabris » Wed Mar 11, 2009 6:15 am To my knowledge, his logical, conscious realization that his mom's in there came later, though.

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Wed Mar 11, 2009 6:41 am FallenTabris wrote: To my knowledge, his logical, conscious realization that his mom's in there came later, though.
